candidate_problems.text: Can 'indexical indispensability' be made non-circular and operationally testable? The conjecture hinges on states like 'this is happening to me now' being functionally irreplaceable by observer-neutral descriptions, but it remains open how to distinguish true indispensability from mere implementation convenience, compression, or limits of current decomposition methods. A key problem is to define interventions or formal criteria that show when replacing indexical states preserves behavior yet destroys a genuinely distinct control variable, rather than just redescribing the same computation.

Is decomposition fragility a real ontological boundary or only a modeling artifact? The conjecture claims that partitioning a self-modeling loop can preserve input-output behavior while eliminating causally usable perspectival states, but this raises the unresolved tension of whether consciousness depends on intrinsic organization or on the grain at which we describe the system. The new problem is to characterize which decompositions are legitimate, what counts as 'external coordination,' and whether there exists a principled invariance criterion separating genuinely irreducible architectures from systems that only appear irreducible under one description.

What exact relation holds between self-referential strange loops and consciousness, rather than sophisticated metacognitive control more generally? The conjecture proposes self-modeling recursive closure as the distinguishing organizational fixed point, but it remains open whether such loops are sufficient, necessary, or merely correlated with conscious experience. This invites the problem of constructing counterexamples: systems with deep self-model recursion but no plausible consciousness, or conscious-like systems with minimal or non-iterated self-reference, to test whether the conjecture isolates consciousness itself or a broader class of adaptive architectures.
